upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a pronounced low-level inversion with a surface-to-850mb lapse rate of only -1.19°C/100m — extremely stable, far below dry adiabatic, indicating a deep marine/thermal inversion capping convection. Critically, the 850mb level (1520m) is 15.6°C, warmer than the surface at 13.8°C, confirming a strong subsidence inversion well below 5000ft. The 700mb flow is light WSW at 8 kts, while 500mb is SW at 14 kts — upper winds are benign and non-threatening, but the inversion effectively kills thermal development today across all inland sites.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area/SF region. All METARs clear with calm surface winds (00000KT) at KSFO, KHAF, KRHV, KAPC, and KWVI as of ~1355Z, confirming a high-pressure dominated morning with no mechanical or precipitation hazards. KHAF dew point spread is 0°C (07/07) suggesting saturated marine air at the coast, though skies are reported CLR — shallow marine moisture present but not yet capping. KRHV at 12°C/6°C with SKC is notably warmer and drier inland, confirming a thermal contrast gradient.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridgeline, NNE-facing launch, thermal/ridge hybrid site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's 1750ft launch works on NW-to-NE thermic cycles that push up the east bay hills; ideal conditions are 8-15 kts from the NW-NNW with developing thermals by late morning. The upper launch benefits most when the sea breeze has yet to fully fill in, allowing thermal streets to develop off the sun-heated valley.
sites.todaySetup: The dominant issue today at the 1750ft launch is the extremely stable atmosphere — the KOAK sounding shows a strong subsidence inversion anchored near 850mb (~1520m MSL, 15.6°C), which places a hard thermal ceiling right at or just above launch elevation. CAPE is zero all day and boundary layer height climbs only modestly from 130m at 08:00 to a peak of ~1315m by 16:00. Surface winds at launch are initially NNE 10-14 kts, rotating to NW-NNW 8-10 kts by late afternoon — directionally usable but with 80m winds running 10-21 kts, there is significant wind shear in the critical launch-to-soaring layer. Cloud cover builds to 100% overcast by early afternoon, further suppressing any thermal development. Tomorrow.io gusts reach 17-18 kts at this elevation by late afternoon, flagging punchy conditions. This is a marginal day: the wind direction and speeds are workable in the morning window but thermals will be weak-to-nonexistent under the inversion, and increasing overcast kills the afternoon.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds NNE 10-14 kts at launch, 80m winds 17-21 kts — strong shear aloft with a low BL height of 130-350m. Sky is clear but the inversion is already in place; no thermal development expected, and the shear layer makes launching risky for anything other than a quick sled ride.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds ease slightly to 11-13 kts NNE, 80m layer drops to 13-17 kts — shear reducing but still present. Cloud cover surges to 100% by 13:00, eliminating solar heating. BL height only reaches 565m by 13:00; thermals capped. Marginal soaring possible on ridge flow but expect glass-off conditions to be muted.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds back to NNW-N, 8-10 kts surface, dropping to ~9-10 kts at 80m by 16:00 — shear nearly gone but winds may be too light for reliable ridge soaring. BL peaks at 1315m at 16:00 but 100% overcast means zero thermal drive. Tomorrow.io gusts up to 17-18 kts flagged for 16-17:00 — punchy and inconsistent under overcast. Not a productive window.

verdict.hazards: Strong wind shear 0-80m in morning (10-21 kts differential), subsidence inversion capping thermals, 100% overcast by noon suppressing solar heating, afternoon gusts to 18 kts

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch is the intermediate training hill at Ed Levin, ideal for short soaring flights in light-moderate NW-to-NNE flow. It needs 8-12 kts on-the-nose NNE to sustain soaring; thermals are possible but limited by the shorter ground clearance.
sites.todaySetup: The 600ft launch faces a compounded problem today: extremely strong early-morning winds (15-16 kts NNE at 10m, 22-24 kts at 80m) that are well above the site's safe operating window in the morning, followed by a total overcast that shuts down thermals for the rest of the day. The HRRR and GFS are in full agreement. BL height starts at only 150-185m, meaning the rotor/mechanical turbulence zone from the ridge above engulfs the launch for several hours. By midday, winds ease to 11-12 kts and the BL climbs to 550-855m, but 100% cloud cover is already established. Tomorrow.io shows gusts only 9-16 kts at this lower elevation in the afternoon, somewhat calmer, but the overcast seals the deal. No viable soaring window today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds 12-16 kts NNE, 80m winds 16-24 kts — significantly over-limit for a training hill. BL height only 150-465m, placing pilots in the mechanical turbulence layer. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease to 11-12 kts NNE, more manageable, but cloud cover hits 78-100% by noon, eliminating thermals. BL climbs to 590-855m but solar drive is gone. Marginal at best for a sled ride in light conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds drop to 8-10 kts NNE-N, and Tomorrow.io gusts are 9-16 kts — near the upper edge for P2. Full overcast continues. A sled ride is physically possible but there is no soaring energy and conditions are mechanically uncertain under the inversion.

verdict.bestWindow: 12–2 PM (only for a sled ride, no soaring)
verdict.hazards: Over-limit winds and shear in morning (80m winds 22-24 kts), full overcast suppressing thermals all afternoon, low BL confining mechanical turbulence to launch zone
verdict.skillLevel: P2+ (sled ride only, experienced P2 with instructor oversight)

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — bunny hill/beginner launch, low elevation, NNE-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 300ft training hill is a beginner slope for P1/P2 students, requiring gentle 5-10 kts NNE winds. It is purely a thermal/sled site with minimal ridge effect; thermals are needed for anything beyond a 30-second glide to LZ.
sites.todaySetup: The 300ft hill actually has the most tractable wind speeds today — surface winds are 1-10 kts with variable direction in the morning, gradually building to 9-10 kts from the NNE-N by early afternoon. However, the 80m layer is running 5-14 kts and directionally variable, indicating mechanical turbulence above the surface layer. The total cloud overcast by noon eliminates any thermal development. BL heights are low (60-990m range), and the inversion from the KOAK sounding caps any usable lift. Tomorrow.io data at this elevation mirrors the 600ft site — gusts to 9-16 kts in the afternoon. This is a usable beginner hill for sled rides in the 10 AM–1 PM window when winds are lightest and most consistent from the NNE, but do not expect soaring.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds very light and variable 1-4 kts (286-349°), essentially calm to cross at times. BL only 60-380m with shear aloft. Suitable for foot-launch practice but not soaring; winds may be too light and variable for reliable kiting.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 5-10 kts from N-NNE by noon-13:00, the best window for beginner flights. Cloud cover building to 100% but this is acceptable for sled rides. BL reaches 645-1250m — technically above launch height, but zero CAPE means no thermal streets.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 7-10 kts NNW-NNE, 100% overcast, Tomorrow.io gusts reaching 13-16 kts by 17:00. Getting punchier under overcast skies with backing wind direction — not ideal for beginners.

verdict.hazards: Light/variable morning winds unreliable for kiting, afternoon gusts to 16 kts exceed P2 comfort zone, variable wind direction throughout day, zero thermal development under overcast
verdict.skillLevel: P2 (sled rides with instructor; P1 students with supervision in the 11AM–12PM window only)

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-to-NW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site driven by the Pacific sea breeze and NW swell winds hitting the coastal bluff. Ideal conditions are 12-22 kts from the WNW-NW; too light and there's no lift, too strong and the site becomes dangerous with rotor below the cliff. Marine layer timing is critical — if the fog/stratus is deep, the sea breeze is suppressed and winds go too light.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is a NO FLY today, and the reason is simple: winds are catastrophically light. The HRRR and GFS are in lockstep showing surface winds at 2-5 kts, peaking at only 5.4 kts from 325° at 14:00. The entire day the 10m wind never exceeds 5.4 kts — well below the 10+ kts needed for ridge soaring at this site. Tomorrow.io corroborates with winds of only 2-4 kts and gusts under 5 kts all day. The KHAF METAR is dead calm (00000KT) with temp/dew spread of 0°C (07/07), indicating saturated marine air right at the coast — but no meaningful gradient to drive a sea breeze. The Half Moon Bay buoy shows only 3.9 kts from 300° with 1.2m/10s swell — minimal wave energy and near-calm offshore. The 80m layer runs 3-11 kts but the surface layer is too light to generate reliable ridge lift, and the site could be in the rotor zone of marginal flow. There is no viable flying window today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds 2-3 kts from E-NE, essentially offshore/calm. No ridge lift possible. 80m winds 3-4 kts. The marine layer is saturated at the coast (KHAF 0°C spread) but not generating any gradient wind.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds shift to NNW-NW and build to 4-5 kts — marginally in the right direction but still far too light for soaring. 100% overcast develops. Tomorrow.io shows only 2-3 kts at 14:00.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds remain 4-5 kts NW-NNW with 80m layer increasing to 6-11 kts — some shear but still below soaring threshold at the surface. Cloud cover 100%. Not flyable.

verdict.hazards: Winds critically below ridge soaring threshold all day, saturated marine air at coast, increasing overcast, no gradient pressure differential to drive sea breeze

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridgeline/peak, W-facing launch, thermal-dominant site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak launches west toward the bay, relying on thermal development off the heated Fremont plain and a light-to-moderate onshore sea breeze to sustain soaring. Ideal winds are 8-15 kts from the W-NW-NNW with strong afternoon thermals. It mirrors Ed Levin dynamics but with a more pronounced peak effect.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same atmospheric story as Ed Levin today: a powerful subsidence inversion at 850mb (~1520m MSL) with zero CAPE caps all thermal development, while 100% overcast builds by early afternoon. The compounding problem here is that the GFS/HRRR 120m wind layer shows values of only 1-10 kts, and by the time the BL reaches useful heights (810m by 14:00, 1315m by 16:00), the sky is fully overcast with no solar drive. Morning winds are NNE 10-14 kts, which is cross-to-on-shore for the west-facing launch — but the 80m shear layer (17-21 kts) creates a dangerous differential for the launch zone. Tomorrow.io shows Mission Peak gusting to 13-16 kts from NE-ENE in the afternoon, which is cross to the best ridge direction. The combination of wrong-direction or sheared winds, inversion-capped thermals, and full overcast produces no viable window today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 10-14 kts surface, 80m winds 17-21 kts — significant shear, wrong direction for the W-facing launch, and BL height only 130-350m. Not launchable in useful conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease to 11-12 kts NNE, 80m drops to 13-15 kts, BL reaches 420-810m — shear reducing but direction still NNE (cross to the W-launch face). Cloud cover hits 100% by 13:00, eliminating thermal drive. Marginal at absolute best.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds back to NNW-N, 8-10 kts surface — closer to usable direction but fully overcast and Tomorrow.io flags gusts to 13-16 kts from NE-ENE. BL peaks at 1315m but zero solar heating. No soaring energy available.

verdict.hazards: Wind direction cross to W-facing launch, 80m shear layer 17-21 kts in morning, subsidence inversion capping thermals, 100% overcast by noon eliminating solar drive, afternoon gusts to 16 kts

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday March 30 looks worse than today — 100% overcast all day, light W-WNW winds 5-8 kts, BL height capped at 95-420m, and 1-2% precip probability by afternoon. The marine layer appears to deepen overnight and not clear. No viable flying window; essentially a clouded-in day with light gradient winds.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same overcast story Monday — 100% cloud, light 3-8 kts W-WNW, BL under 475m all day. GFS and HRRR agree completely. Not flyable.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday brings full overcast, light 2-7 kts WNW, and the deepening marine layer keeping BL heights low (140-570m). No soaring, sled rides only if winds build to 5+ kts by afternoon. Marginal for beginner operations at best.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Monday could be marginally better for Mussel Rock — winds build to 6-8 kts W-WNW at 10m and 7-10 kts at 80m, closer to the soaring threshold. However, 100% overcast all day and Tomorrow.io showing only 5-6 kt gusts suggests the sea breeze remains weak. Borderline marginal — watch for afternoon wind fill.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Monday is not flyable — full overcast, light W-WNW 5-8 kts, BL under 420m. The 120m layer reaches 14.8 kts by 14:00 which shows some upper flow, but surface conditions remain suppressed under the marine layer. No thermal development possible.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday March 31 shows signs of clearing with cloud cover dropping from 100% in the morning to 55% by 16:00 and 10% by 18:00. Winds are light (0.5-8 kts variable, then NW 7-8 kts by afternoon) and CAPE appears at 20 J/kg by 14:00. BL heights climb to 1000-1115m by midday. This could be a transitional day with weak thermals developing late afternoon — worth monitoring.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday March 31 mirrors the 1750ft forecast with partial clearing by late afternoon. Light winds and modest BL heights suggest weak thermal activity. The 16:00 window with 55% cloud cover and 7-8 kts NW could produce light soaring. Marginal but improving trend.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Tuesday March 31 has W-WSW winds building to 7-9 kts at 10m and 8-10 kts at 80m — approaching soaring threshold. Partial clearing by 16:00 (13% cloud). Could be marginal for Mussel Rock if sea breeze fills in. Worth checking updated HRRR on Tuesday morning.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Tuesday shows BL heights climbing to 715-995m with partial clearing and 27% precip probability — unstable mix. Light winds (1-5 kts variable) through midday with NW 5-12 kts at 80m by afternoon. Marginal thermal potential but wind direction and precip risk complicate the picture.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday offers gradual clearing with light winds; BL heights reach 1000m+ by afternoon suggesting thermal development possible if skies clear as forecast. Light precip chance (10-25%) is the main risk. Monitor.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday April 1 looks like the best day of the 4-day window — BL heights climb to 1535-2325m, CAPE builds to 10-40 J/kg, winds SW-WSW 3-11 kts at surface and 8-18 kts at 80m by afternoon. Thermals should be moderate. The SW flow (235-249°) is somewhat cross but not prohibitive. Watch the 28-32% precip probability — could indicate convective activity. Strong thermal day with the trade-off of possible afternoon showers.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday April 1 echoes the 1750ft forecast with excellent BL development (590-2325m) and CAPE 10-40 J/kg. SW winds 3-11 kts. Best thermal flying day of the week, but precip probability (20-28%) and SW direction need monitoring. P3 weather.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Wednesday April 1 shows a dramatic shift — winds build to 12-14 kts from SSW-SW by afternoon, with 80m winds 15-16 kts. BL reaches 795-995m, CAPE 10-20 J/kg. The SSW direction is sub-optimal for Mussel Rock (prefers WNW) and 20-34% precip risk adds uncertainty. Could be flyable but direction is marginal.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Wednesday April 1 is the standout day for Mission Peak — BL heights of 1435-2250m, CAPE up to 40 J/kg, winds building to WSW 9-12 kts at 10m and 14-17 kts at 80m by afternoon. Strong thermals likely but precip risk (21-28%) and upper-level instability require careful assessment. P3+ day.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday April 1 brings strong thermal development with BL heights over 2300m and CAPE to 40 J/kg — this would be too aggressive a day for the bunny hill. Afternoon SW winds 8-11 kts with gusts are above the beginner threshold. Not recommended for P1/P2 operations.

watchlist: Subsidence inversion anchored near 850mb (1520m MSL) kills thermal development at all inland sites today — Ed Levin and Mission Peak are effectively flying in a box
Marine air fully saturated at KHAF (0°C temp/dew spread, 00000KT calm) with SST only 12.2°C — sea breeze gradient absent today, explaining Mussel Rock's dead-calm conditions
Tomorrow.io gusts at Ed Levin 1750ft reach 17-18 kts by 16-17:00 despite light surface readings — punchy rotor/mechanical turbulence possible under overcast in late afternoon
100% overcast forecast to persist through Monday at all sites — extended poor flying stretch ahead
Wednesday April 1 shows CAPE building to 40 J/kg with 28-32% precip probability — potential for convective showers in the afternoon; plan early flights if flying Wednesday
Buoy 46012 (Half Moon Bay) SST at 12.2°C is cold — expect slow or no marine layer burn-off pattern to persist through the weekend
Wind shear between 10m and 80m levels is extreme this morning at Ed Levin (10 vs 21 kts) — dangerous for any launch attempt at 1750ft before 11 AM
tldr: Today is largely a wash for Bay Area paragliding — a powerful subsidence inversion is capping thermals at all inland sites and the coastal sea breeze is completely absent, leaving Mussel Rock with 2-5 kt winds all day. Ed Levin's lower hills have a narrow marginal window around 11 AM–1 PM for sled rides in light NNE flow, but don't expect any soaring. Your best bet this week is Wednesday April 1 when BL heights climb above 2000m and thermals fire — just watch the afternoon precip risk.
